summary: Intent-aware endpoint security that intervenes before risky human or AI-agent actions complete.
editor_note: The bet is that endpoint security should read intent, not just processes. Ent watches human and AI-agent activity across apps, AI tools, and workflows, then can nudge or block before a risky action completes. Compared with EDR tools that reconstruct what happened after the breach, this is prevention at the moment of work. The question is whether enterprises will trust a new agent this close to the desktop.

product_evidence.claim: The official site positions Ent as an intent-aware workspace security platform for human, AI, and application activity across enterprise workflows.
The platform page says Ent runs as one AI-native agent across endpoints, reading intent behind actions and stepping in with plain-language guidance or hard blocks before risky work completes.
Ent lists integrations with EDR, SIEM, SOAR, IAM, collaboration, and service-management systems including CrowdStrike, Defender, Splunk, Okta, Google Workspace, Jira, ServiceNow, and Slack.
The funding announcement says Ent is generally available for Windows, macOS, Linux, and browser-extension deployments and is backed by a $100M seed round led by Decibel.

market_context.primary_user: Enterprise CISOs, security operations teams, insider-risk teams, and AI platform owners responsible for endpoint and agent activity.
market_context.differentiation: Ent focuses on interpreting user and agent intent at the endpoint, then intervening before an action completes rather than only reconstructing incidents after detection.
market_context.why_now: AI agents and faster exploit discovery compress the response window for security teams, making prevention and policy enforcement at the work surface more valuable.

risks.claim: The open question is whether enterprises will accept an endpoint agent that interprets intent without creating privacy, false-positive, or workforce-trust backlash.
risks.basis: VentureDex editorial assessment based on official product pages and current funding-source review.

investors: Decibel, Sequoia Capital, Crosspoint Capital, Craft Ventures, Shield Capital, Felicis, In-Q-Tel
